name: acacia nilotica
mechanism: Primary activity is astringency from high tannin content (protein-precipitating effects on mucosa). Polyphenols such as catechin/quercetin contribute antioxidant and anti-inflammatory signaling seen mainly in preclinical models.
effects: No direct effects data. Contextual inference: Folk medicine.. No direct mechanismofaction data. Contextual inference: Folk medicine.. Folk medicine. Folk medicine
contraindications: High doses of bark decoction can irritate the digestive tract and cause constipation or nausea
Avoid during pregnancy or lactation due to potential anti-fertility effects
safetyNotes: Most use is oral/astringent and can cause GI irritation, nausea, or constipation at higher intake. Tannin-rich preparations may reduce absorption of oral drugs and minerals (interaction category: oral medications/supplements, especially iron). Avoid use in pregnancy/lactation due to limited safety data.
description: Acacia nilotica is an astringent medicinal tree used in traditional systems for oral, skin, and gastrointestinal applications; its bark and pods are rich in tannins and other polyphenols.

therapeuticUses: Oral health (reducing plaque and gingival inflammation)
Gastrointestinal disorders such as diarrhea and dysentery
Liver protection and blood sugar regulation
